title: Basic Inheritance Syntax
ques: How do you denote inheritance in C#? Create a class `Employee` and a subclass `Programmer`.
explanation: Use the colon `:` symbol. `class Derived : Base`. `Programmer` inherits all non-private members of `Employee`.

title: The 'base' Keyword
ques: Use the **`base`** keyword to call the constructor of the base class from a derived class constructor.
explanation: `base` is used to access members of the base class. `base(args)` calls the parent constructor.

title: Sealed Classes
ques: What does the **`sealed`** keyword do? Try to inherit from a sealed class `FinalClass`.
explanation: A `sealed` class cannot be inherited. It prevents other classes from deriving from it.

title: Method Hiding (new)
ques: Demonstrate **Method Hiding** using the `new` keyword to redefine a base class method in the derived class.
explanation: Using `new` explicitly tells the compiler that you intend to hide the base class member, suppressing the warning.

title: Polymorphism with Array
ques: Create an array of base class `Shape` pointers containing `Circle` and `Square` objects. iterate and call a virtual method `Draw`.
explanation: The reference variable `s` (type `Shape`) can point to any derived class object. The overridden `Draw` method of the actual object type is called at runtime.

title: Interface Inheritance
ques: Can a class implement **multiple interfaces**? Create a class `SmartPhone` implementing `ICamera` and `IPhone`.
explanation: C# does not support multiple inheritance of classes, but it supports implementing multiple interfaces.

title: Explicit Interface Implementation
ques: If two interfaces have a method with the same name, how do you implement them distinctily? Using **Explicit Interface Implementation**.
explanation: Prefix the method name with the interface name. These methods are only accessible via an instance of the interface, not the class.

title: is and as Operators
ques: Use **`is`** to check if an object is a string, and **`as`** to safely cast an object to a string.
explanation: `is` returns true/false. `as` returns the casted object or `null` if the cast fails (no exception).

title: Abstract Properties
ques: Can define an **abstract property**? Show syntax in an abstract class `Person`.
explanation: Yes, abstract properties must be overridden in derived classes using the `override` keyword.

title: Calling Base Method
ques: Override a method `Greet` in derived class but call the **base** class implementation inside it.
explanation: `base.MethodName()` calls the method as defined in the base class.
